docs.title: CPS HHBK Temperature Monitoring System
docs.text: Welcome to the documentation for the CPS HHBK project - a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) designed for temperature monitoring and visual feedback using Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project implements an intelligent temperature monitoring system that reads ambient temperature using a KY-001 sensor (DS18B20) and provides visual feedback through RGB LEDs based on configurable temperature thresholds.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Real-time Temperature Monitoring: Continuous temperature reading using the KY-001 (DS18B20) sensor
  • Visual Feedback System: Color-coded LED indicators for different temperature ranges
  • Modular Architecture: Clean separation between sensors, actors, and application logic
  • Raspberry Pi Based: Designed to run on Raspberry Pi hardware with GPIO support

docs.title: Temperature Ranges
docs.text:
The system categorizes temperature into three zones:

Temperature Range LED Color Status < 21°C Green Cool/Comfortable 21-25°C Yellow Warm ≥ 26°C Red Hot

Project Purpose

This project was developed as part of Block 2 CPS coursework at HHBK, demonstrating practical application of cyber-physical systems concepts including sensor integration, actuator control, and embedded systems programming.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: This guide will help you set up and run the CPS HHBK temperature monitoring system on your Raspberry Pi.

docs.title: Hardware Requirements
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi (tested on Pi 3/4/Zero W)
  • KY-001 Temperature Sensor (DS18B20 based)
  • 3x LEDs (Green, Yellow, Red)
  • 3x 220Ω Resistors (for LEDs)
  • Breadboard and jumper wires
  • Power supply for Raspberry Pi

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi OS (formerly Raspbian)
  • Python 3.7 or higher
  • Internet connection for initial setup

GPIO Access Required

This project requires GPIO access. Ensure your user is in the gpio group: 

sudo usermod -a -G gpio $USER

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Maxjr2/cps-hhbk.git
cd cps-hhbk

docs.title: Step 2: Install Python Dependencies
docs.text:
Install the required Python packages:

pip3 install gpiozero

Virtual Environment

It's recommended to use a virtual environment: 

python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install gpiozero

docs.title: Step 3: Enable 1-Wire Interface
docs.text:
The KY-001 sensor uses the 1-Wire protocol. Enable it on your Raspberry Pi:

Using raspi-configManual Configuration 
sudo raspi-config

Navigate to: Interface Options → 1-Wire → Enable

Edit /boot/config.txt: 

sudo nano /boot/config.txt

Add the following line: 

dtoverlay=w1-gpio

Reboot your Raspberry Pi: 

sudo reboot

docs.title: Step 4: Verify 1-Wire Setup
docs.text:
After reboot, verify the 1-Wire interface is working:

ls /sys/bus/w1/devices/

You should see a device starting with 28- (your temperature sensor).

Troubleshooting

If you don't see the device, check your wiring and ensure the 1-Wire module is loaded: 

lsmod | grep w1

docs.title: Hardware Setup
docs.text: Before running the software, you need to wire up the components. See the Hardware Setup Guide for detailed wiring instructions.

docs.title: Quick Wiring Reference
docs.text:
KY-001 Temperature Sensor:

  • VCC → 3.3V
  • GND → Ground
  • DATA → GPIO 4 (default 1-Wire pin)

LEDs (with 220Ω resistors):

  • Green LED → GPIO 17
  • Yellow LED → GPIO 27
  • Red LED → GPIO 22

Update GPIO Pins

The current implementation has placeholder GPIO pins. You'll need to update actors/led.py with your actual pin numbers: 

green = LED(17)
yellow = LED(27)
red = LED(22)

docs.title: First Run: Self-Test
docs.text:
The application includes a self-test that cycles through all LED colors:

python3 main.py

You should see the LEDs cycle through green → yellow → red → off.

docs.title: Normal Operation
docs.text:
To run the temperature monitoring system:

# In main.py or Python shell
from main import get_sensor_state
get_sensor_state()

The LED will change based on the current temperature reading.

docs.title: Test Temperature Sensor
docs.text:
You can manually read the temperature:

cat /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*/w1_slave

Or using Python:

python3
>>> import sensors.ky001 as temp
>>> temp_c, temp_f = temp.read_temp()
>>> print(f"Temperature: {temp_c}°C / {temp_f}°F")

docs.title: Test LEDs
docs.text:
Test individual LED control:

python3
>>> import actors.led as led
>>> led.set_led("green")   # Should turn on green LED
>>> led.set_led("yellow")  # Should turn on yellow LED
>>> led.set_led("red")     # Should turn on red LED
>>> led.selftest()         # Cycles through all colors

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: This guide helps diagnose and resolve common issues with the CPS HHBK temperature monitoring system.

docs.title: Quick Diagnostics
docs.text:
Start here for rapid issue identification:

graph TD
    A[Issue?] --> B{Power On?}
    B -->|No| C[Check Power Supply]
    B -->|Yes| D{Sensor Working?}
    D -->|No| E[Check Sensor Section]
    D -->|Yes| F{LEDs Working?}
    F -->|No| G[Check LED Section]
    F -->|Yes| H[Check Software Section]

docs.title: Sensor Not Detected
docs.text:
Symptom: No device appears in /sys/bus/w1/devices/

Diagnosis: 

# Check for 1-Wire devices
ls /sys/bus/w1/devices/

# Expected: 28-XXXXXXXXXXXX
# Actual: Only w1_bus_master1

Solutions:

Check 1-Wire EnabledVerify WiringTest SensorCheck Kernel Messages

Verify 1-Wire interface is enabled:

# Check if modules are loaded
lsmod | grep w1

# Should see:
# w1_gpio
# w1_therm

If not loaded: 

sudo raspi-config
# Interface Options → 1-Wire → Enable
sudo reboot

Check physical connections:

  • VCC → 3.3V (not 5V!)
  • GND → Ground
  • DATA → GPIO 4 (Pin 7)
  • 4.7kΩ pull-up resistor between DATA and 3.3V

Test if sensor is functional:

  1. Try sensor on different GPIO pin
  2. Check with multimeter: VCC to GND should show ~3.3V
  3. Try different sensor if available

Look for errors in system logs:

dmesg | grep w1
# Look for error messages
# Manual module loading
sudo modprobe w1-gpio
sudo modprobe w1-therm

docs.title: Sensor Returns Invalid Data
docs.text:
Symptom: CRC check fails, YES never appears

Diagnosis: 

cat /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*/w1_slave

# Bad output:
# 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 : crc=00 NO

Solutions:

  1. Check pull-up resistor
  2. Must be 4.7kΩ ±10%
  3. Connect between DATA and VCC

  4. Some modules have built-in resistor

  5. Reduce cable length

  6. Long cables (>3m) can cause issues
  7. Use shielded cable for long runs

  8. Consider different pull-up value for long cables

  9. Check power supply

  10. Weak power causes unreliable readings
  11. Use quality power supply (2.5A+ for Pi)
  12. Verify 3.3V rail voltage

docs.title: Temperature Readings Incorrect
docs.text:
Symptom: Sensor reads but values are wrong

Diagnosis: 

import sensors.ky001
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
print(f"{temp_c}°C / {temp_f}°F")
# Reports 85°C (power-on reset value)
# Or unusual values

Solutions:

Power-On Reset ValueCalibration OffsetValidate RangeTest Reference Points

85°C is the DS18B20 power-on default:

# Wait for first conversion
import time
time.sleep(1)  # Wait 1 second
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()

Apply calibration if consistently off:

TEMP_OFFSET = -0.5  # Adjust as needed
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
temp_c += TEMP_OFFSET
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0

Check if reading is physically possible:

temp_c = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]
if not (-50 <= temp_c <= 100):
print(f"Invalid temperature: {temp_c}°C")
# Retry or use last known good value

Verify accuracy at known temperatures:

  • Ice water: Should read ~0°C
  • Room temperature: Should read ~20-25°C
  • Body temperature: Should read ~37°C

docs.title: Sensor Read Hangs
docs.text:
Symptom: read_temp() never returns, program freezes

Cause: CRC validation loop has no timeout

Solutions:

Implement TimeoutCheck ConnectionAdd Retry Limit

Add timeout to sensor reading:

import sensors.ky001
import signal
class TimeoutError(Exception):
pass
def timeout_handler(signum, frame):
raise TimeoutError("Sensor timeout")
signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, timeout_handler)
signal.alarm(5)  # 5 second timeout
try:
temp = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
signal.alarm(0)  # Cancel alarm
except TimeoutError:
print("Sensor read timed out")

Verify sensor is physically connected:

ls /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*
# If no output, sensor disconnected

Modify read_temp() to limit retries:

# In sensors/ky001.py
def read_temp_safe(max_retries=10):
lines = read_temp_raw()
attempts = 0
while lines[0].strip()[-3:] != 'YES':
if attempts >= max_retries:
raise TimeoutError("CRC validation failed")
time.sleep(0.2)
lines = read_temp_raw()
attempts += 1
# ... rest of function

docs.title: No LED Response
docs.text:
Symptom: LEDs don't turn on

Diagnosis: 

import actors.led
actors.led.selftest()
# No LEDs light up

Solutions:

Check GPIO Pin ConfigurationVerify WiringTest LED PolarityCheck PermissionsTest Directly

Problem: LED objects created without pin numbers

# Current (broken):
green = LED()
# Fixed:
green = LED(17)

Update actors/led.py: 

from gpiozero import LED
green = LED(17)   # GPIO 17
yellow = LED(27)  # GPIO 27
red = LED(22)     # GPIO 22

Check LED connections:

  • Anode (long leg) → Resistor → GPIO
  • Cathode (short leg) → Ground
  • 220Ω resistor present
  • Correct GPIO pins used

LEDs only work in one direction:

Correct:  GPIO → Resistor → Anode → Cathode → GND
Wrong:    GPIO → Resistor → Cathode → Anode → GND

Fix: Rotate LED 180°

Verify GPIO access:

# Add user to gpio group
sudo usermod -a -G gpio $USER
# Logout and login again
# Or:
newgrp gpio

Bypass LED module:

from gpiozero import LED
test_led = LED(17)
test_led.on()
# LED should light
test_led.off()

docs.title: Wrong LED Lights Up
docs.text:
Symptom: Calling set_led("green") lights yellow or red

Cause: GPIO pin mismatch between code and wiring

Solutions:

  1. Option A: Fix Code 

    # Update actors/led.py to match wiring
green = LED(actual_green_pin)
yellow = LED(actual_yellow_pin)
red = LED(actual_red_pin)

  2. Option B: Fix Wiring

  3. Rewire LEDs to match code
  4. GPIO 17 → Green
  5. GPIO 27 → Yellow

  6. GPIO 22 → Red

  7. Document Current Config 

    # actors/led.py
# LED Configuration:
# Green:  GPIO 17 (Physical Pin 11)
# Yellow: GPIO 27 (Physical Pin 13)
# Red:    GPIO 22 (Physical Pin 15)
green = LED(17)
yellow = LED(27)
red = LED(22)

docs.title: LEDs Very Dim
docs.text:
Symptom: LEDs work but are barely visible

Solutions:

Wrong Resistor ValuePower Supply IssueLong Wires

Problem: Resistor too large (>500Ω)

Solution: Use 220Ω resistors

Current = (3.3V - 2.0V) / R

With 1kΩ:  I = 1.3mA  (very dim)
With 220Ω: I = 6mA    (good brightness)
With 100Ω: I = 13mA   (bright)

Problem: Insufficient current from power supply

Check: 

vcgencmd get_throttled
# 0x0 = OK
# Other = Under-voltage detected

Solution: Use quality 2.5A+ power supply

Problem: Voltage drop over wire length

Solution: - Use shorter wires - Use thicker gauge wire - Place breadboard closer to Pi

docs.title: Multiple LEDs On Simultaneously
docs.text:
Symptom: More than one LED lights at once

Cause: Logic error in set_led()

Check Implementation: 

def set_led(color):
# These lines MUST be present:
green.off()
yellow.off()
red.off()
# Then activate only one
if color == "green":
green.on()
elif color == "yellow":
yellow.on()
elif color == "red":
red.on()

Verify: 

import actors.led
# Test each color individually
actors.led.set_led("green")
# Only green should be on
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
# Only yellow should be on

docs.title: Module Import Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: ImportError: No module named 'gpiozero'

Solution: 

# Install gpiozero
pip3 install gpiozero

# Or with apt
sudo apt install python3-gpiozero

docs.title: Module Not Found: sensors/actors
docs.text:
Symptom: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'sensors'

Solutions:

Wrong DirectoryPYTHONPATH IssueMissing init.py

Run from project root: 

cd /path/to/cps-hhbk
python3 main.py

Add project to Python path: 

export PYTHONPATH="${PYTHONPATH}:/path/to/cps-hhbk"
python3 main.py

Ensure package files exist: 

touch sensors/__init__.py
touch actors/__init__.py

docs.title: Permission Denied Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied

Solutions:

GPIO Access1-Wire AccessRun as Root (Not Recommended)

Add user to gpio group: 

sudo usermod -a -G gpio $USER
logout  # Then login again

Check file permissions: 

ls -l /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*/w1_slave
# Should be readable by all
# If not:
sudo chmod a+r /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*/w1_slave

Last resort only: 

sudo python3 main.py

docs.title: Logic Error in main.py
docs.text:
Symptom: LED behavior doesn't match temperature

Issue: Unreachable condition in main.py:

# BROKEN CODE:
if temperature < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temperature >= 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
elif temperature >= 31:  # ← UNREACHABLE!
actors.led.set_led("red")

Fix: 

# CORRECTED CODE:
temp_c = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]
if temp_c < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temp_c < 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
else:  # >= 26
actors.led.set_led("red")

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Won't Boot
docs.text:
Symptoms: - No LED activity on Pi - No video output - Red power LED on, green activity LED off

Solutions:

Power SupplySD CardShort Circuit

Problem: Insufficient power

Check: - Use 2.5A+ supply - Official Raspberry Pi power supply recommended - Quality USB cable (not thin charging cable)

Test: 

# After booting, check throttling
vcgencmd get_throttled

Problem: Corrupted or missing OS

Solutions: 1. Re-image SD card with Raspberry Pi Imager 2. Use quality SD card (Class 10, A1 rating) 3. Check SD card is fully inserted

Problem: Hardware short circuit

Diagnosis: 1. Disconnect all external connections 2. Try booting with only power 3. If boots, reconnect components one at a time

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Shuts Down Randomly
docs.text:
Symptom: Pi reboots or shuts down unexpectedly

Solutions:

Under-VoltageOverheatingSD Card Corruption

Check: 

vcgencmd get_throttled
# 0x50000 = Under-voltage occurred

Fix: - Use better power supply (2.5A minimum, 3A recommended) - Shorter/thicker power cable - Remove high-power USB devices

Check: 

vcgencmd measure_temp
# Should be < 80°C

Fix: - Add heatsinks - Improve ventilation - Use cooling fan - Reduce CPU load

Symptoms: - Random errors - File system read-only

Fix: 

# Backup and re-image SD card
# Enable journaling

docs.title: Actors Overview
docs.text: This section documents all actor modules used in the CPS HHBK system for controlling physical output devices.

docs.title: What Are Actors?
docs.text:
In cyber-physical systems, actors (also called actuators) are output devices that receive digital commands from the computer system and produce physical effects in the real world.

graph LR
    A[Software] -->|Digital Command| B[Actor]
    B -->|Physical Action| C[Environment]
    C -.->|Feedback| D[Sensors]
    D -->|Data| A

docs.title: Visual Output
docs.text: Actor Type Interface Function Status LED Controller RGB LEDs GPIO Visual temperature indication ✅ Implemented

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
All actor modules in the actors/ directory follow a consistent pattern:

# actors/<actor_name>.py
# Hardware initialization (module level)
device = HardwareInterface()
def control_<action>(parameters):
"""High-level control function"""
# Validate parameters
# Execute hardware commands
# Update internal state
def selftest():
"""Hardware verification routine"""
# Test all capabilities
# Return status

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Encapsulation: Hide GPIO/hardware complexity
  2. State Management: Track and validate device states
  3. Safety: Ensure safe operation (no conflicts, limits)
  4. Testing: Include self-test capabilities
  5. Documentation: Clear API and behavior

docs.title: GPIO Output
docs.text:
The LED controller uses GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pins for digital output:

Characteristics:

  • Binary states (HIGH/LOW, ON/OFF)
  • 3.3V logic level on Raspberry Pi
  • Software-controlled through gpiozero library
  • Fast switching (microsecond response)

Implementation:

from gpiozero import LED
led = LED(17)  # GPIO 17
led.on()       # Set HIGH (3.3V)
led.off()      # Set LOW (0V)

docs.title: PWM (Potential Future Use)
docs.text:
For variable brightness or motor speed control:

from gpiozero import PWMLED
led = PWMLED(17)
led.value = 0.5  # 50% brightness

docs.title: Other Interfaces
docs.text:
Future actors might use:

  • I2C: Servo controllers, displays
  • SPI: High-speed DACs, motor drivers
  • Serial: Modems, specialized devices

docs.title: Actor Control Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Application Logic] -->|set_led color| B[LED Module]
    B -->|Validate Color| C{Valid?}
    C -->|No| D[Ignore/Error]
    C -->|Yes| E[Turn Off All LEDs]
    E --> F[Activate Selected LED]
    F --> G[Update GPIO State]
    G --> H[Physical LED Lights]

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
The LED controller uses module-level state:

# actors/led.py
green = LED()   # State stored in LED objects
yellow = LED()
red = LED()

Advantages: - Simple implementation - No explicit state tracking needed - Hardware holds the state

Disadvantages: - Can't query state without reading GPIO - State lost on program exit - No history or logging

docs.title: Improved State Management
docs.text:
For more complex systems:

from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
class LEDColor(Enum):
GREEN = "green"
YELLOW = "yellow"
RED = "red"
OFF = None
class LEDController:
def __init__(self):
self.leds = {
LEDColor.GREEN: LED(17),
LEDColor.YELLOW: LED(27),
LEDColor.RED: LED(22)
}
self._current_state = LEDColor.OFF
@property
def state(self) -> LEDColor:
"""Get current LED state"""
return self._current_state
def set_color(self, color: LEDColor) -> None:
"""Set LED with state tracking"""
# Turn off all
for led in self.leds.values():
led.off()
# Activate selected
if color != LEDColor.OFF:
self.leds[color].on()
# Update state
self._current_state = color

docs.title: Adding New Actors
docs.text: To add a new actor to the system:

docs.title: Step 1: Create Actor Module
docs.text:
# actors/new_actor.py
"""
New Actor Module

docs.title: Electrical Safety
docs.text:
Current Limiting:

Always use current-limiting resistors with LEDs:

GPIO → Resistor → LED → Ground

Power Limits:

Raspberry Pi GPIO limitations:

  • Per Pin: 16mA maximum
  • All Pins: 50mA total recommended
  • Protection: No over-current protection built-in!

GPIO Damage Risk

Exceeding current limits can permanently damage GPIO pins!

docs.title: Software Safety
docs.text:
Prevent Conflicts:

def set_led(color):
# Turn off ALL first to prevent multiple LEDs on
green.off()
yellow.off()
red.off()
# Then activate only one
if color == "green":
green.on()

Cleanup on Exit:

import atexit
def cleanup():
"""Turn off all actors on exit"""
set_led(None)
atexit.register(cleanup)

Exception Handling:

try:
set_led("green")
except Exception as e:
print(f"LED control failed: {e}")
# Safe fallback
cleanup()

docs.title: Self-Test
docs.text:

def selftest():
"""Run LED self-test sequence"""

sleep(1)
set_led("yellow")
sleep(1)
set_led("red")
sleep(1)
set_led(None)

Purpose: - Verify hardware connections - Test all functions - Visual confirmation - Debugging aid

docs.title: LED Controller
docs.text: The LED controller module provides visual feedback for the temperature monitoring system using three color-coded LEDs (green, yellow, red).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The LED controller is an actor that translates temperature state into visual indicators, making the system status immediately apparent to users.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
• Visual Feedback: Instant temperature status indication
  • User Interface: No display needed
  • Color Coding: Intuitive temperature ranges
  • Simple Control: Easy software interface

docs.title: LED Specifications
docs.text:
LED Color Forward Voltage Current GPIO Pin Resistor Cool Status Green ~2.0V 6-8mA GPIO 17 220Ω Warm Status Yellow ~2.0V 6-8mA GPIO 27 220Ω Hot Status Red ~1.8V 6-8mA GPIO 22 220Ω

GPIO Pin Configuration Required

The current code has empty LED() constructors. You must update actors/led.py with your actual GPIO pin numbers: 

green = LED(17)   # Not LED()
yellow = LED(27)
red = LED(22)

docs.text:
Raspberry Pi                     LEDs

GPIO 17 ───┬─── 220Ω ───┬──▶│──┬─── GND
           │              Green  │
GPIO 27 ───┼─── 220Ω ───┬──▶│──┤
           │             Yellow  │
GPIO 22 ───┘─── 220Ω ───┬──▶│──┤
                          Red    │
                                 │
                            Common GND

docs.title: Wiring
docs.text:
See Wiring Guide for detailed connection instructions.

Quick Reference:

  1. Connect LED anode (long leg) → 220Ω resistor → GPIO pin
  2. Connect LED cathode (short leg) → Ground
  3. Verify polarity (LEDs only work one direction!)

docs.title: Global Objects
docs.text:
from gpiozero import LED
green = LED()   # GPIO pin needed!
yellow = LED()  # GPIO pin needed!
red = LED()     # GPIO pin needed!

Configuration Required

These LED objects are created without pin numbers. Update before use: 

green = LED(17)
yellow = LED(27)
red = LED(22)

docs.title: set_led(color)
docs.text:
Set LED to specified color, turning off all others.

Signature: 

def set_led(color: str) -> None

Parameters: - color (str): LED color to activate - "green": Activate green LED - "yellow": Activate yellow LED - "red": Activate red LED - None or other: Turn off all LEDs

Returns: - None

Behavior:

  1. Turns off all three LEDs
  2. Turns on the specified LED (if valid)
  3. Invalid colors result in all LEDs off

Example: 

from actors import led
# Turn on green LED
led.set_led("green")
# Turn on yellow LED
led.set_led("yellow")
# Turn on red LED
led.set_led("red")
# Turn off all LEDs
led.set_led(None)

Implementation: 

def set_led(color):
"""Sets the LED color based on the input string."""
green.off()
yellow.off()
red.off()
if color == "green":
green.on()
elif color == "yellow":
yellow.on()
elif color == "red":
red.on()

Algorithm:

  1. Reset State: Turn off all LEDs to prevent multiple on
  2. Validate Input: Check if color is valid
  3. Activate: Turn on only the specified LED
  4. Graceful Handling: Invalid input = all off

docs.title: selftest()
docs.text:
Run LED self-test sequence to verify hardware.

Signature: 

def selftest() -> None

Parameters: - None

Returns: - None

Behavior:

Cycles through all LED colors with 1-second delays:

  1. Green ON for 1 second
  2. Yellow ON for 1 second
  3. Red ON for 1 second
  4. All OFF

Example: 

from actors import led
# Run hardware test
led.selftest()

Implementation: 

def selftest():
"""Runs a self-test by cycling through the LED colors."""
set_led("green")
sleep(1)
set_led("yellow")
sleep(1)
set_led("red")
sleep(1)
set_led(None)

Use Cases:

  • Initial Setup: Verify wiring is correct
  • Debugging: Check LEDs respond to commands
  • Demonstration: Show system capabilities
  • Health Check: Periodic hardware verification

docs.title: Current Implementation
docs.text:
From main.py:

import actors.led
import sensors.ky001
def get_sensor_state():
"""Reads temperature and sets LED based on range."""
temperature = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
if temperature < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temperature >= 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
elif temperature >= 31:
actors.led.set_led("red")
# Run self-test at startup
actors.led.selftest()

Logic Issue

The condition elif temperature >= 31 is unreachable because elif temperature >= 26 catches all values ≥26. This should be fixed:

def get_sensor_state():
temperature = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]  # Get Celsius only
if temperature < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temperature < 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
else:  # >= 26
actors.led.set_led("red")

docs.title: Continuous Monitoring
docs.text:
import time
import actors.led
import sensors.ky001
def monitor_temperature():
"""Continuous temperature monitoring with LED feedback"""
actors.led.selftest()  # Verify hardware
while True:
try:
temp_c = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]
# Update LED based on temperature
if temp_c < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temp_c < 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
else:
actors.led.set_led("red")
print(f"Temperature: {temp_c}°C")
time.sleep(5)  # Update every 5 seconds
except KeyboardInterrupt:
print("\nShutting down...")
actors.led.set_led(None)
break
except Exception as e:
print(f"Error: {e}")
# Flash error
for _ in range(3):
actors.led.set_led("red")
time.sleep(0.2)
actors.led.set_led(None)
time.sleep(0.2)
if __name__ == "__main__":
monitor_temperature()

docs.title: Why gpiozero?
docs.text:
The gpiozero library provides:

  • Simple API: High-level, Pythonic interface
  • Abstraction: Hides low-level GPIO complexity
  • Safety: Automatic cleanup
  • Portability: Works across Raspberry Pi models
  • Documentation: Well-documented and maintained

docs.title: LED Class
docs.text:
from gpiozero import LED
# Create LED object
led = LED(17)  # GPIO 17
# Control methods
led.on()       # Turn on (GPIO HIGH, 3.3V)
led.off()      # Turn off (GPIO LOW, 0V)
led.toggle()   # Switch state
led.blink()    # Automatic blinking
# Properties
led.is_lit     # True if on, False if off
led.pin        # Pin object
led.value      # 0 or 1

docs.title: Alternative: PWMLED
docs.text:
For variable brightness:

from gpiozero import PWMLED
led = PWMLED(17)
# Brightness control (0.0 to 1.0)
led.value = 0.0   # Off
led.value = 0.25  # 25% brightness
led.value = 0.5   # 50% brightness
led.value = 1.0   # Full brightness
# Pulse effect
led.pulse()

docs.title: LED Doesn't Light
docs.text:
Possible Causes:

  1. Incorrect Polarity
  2. LED is backwards (anode/cathode swapped)

  3. Solution: Rotate LED 180°

  4. Missing Resistor

  5. LED might be damaged from overcurrent

  6. Solution: Always use 220Ω resistor

  7. Wrong GPIO Pin

  8. Code doesn't match wiring

  9. Solution: Verify pin numbers in code and hardware

  10. No GPIO Configuration

  11. LED() without pin number

  12. Solution: Update code with LED(17), etc.

  13. Faulty LED

  14. LED is burned out
  15. Solution: Test with multimeter or replace

Debugging Steps:

# Test LED directly
from gpiozero import LED
green = LED(17)
green.on()   # Should light up
# If not, try different pin
test = LED(17)
test.on()
# Check GPIO state
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)  # Force high

docs.title: Wrong LED Lights Up
docs.text:
Cause: GPIO pin assignments don't match wiring

Solution:

  1. Check physical wiring
  2. Update code to match hardware
  3. Or rewire to match code

docs.title: Multiple LEDs On
docs.text:
Cause: Logic error in set_led()

Check: 

# Should turn off all before activating one
def set_led(color):
green.off()   # This line is critical
yellow.off()  # This line is critical
red.off()     # This line is critical
# Then activate only one
if color == "green":
green.on()

docs.title: All LEDs Very Dim
docs.text:
Possible Causes:

  1. Wrong Resistor Value
  2. Too high resistance (>1kΩ)

  3. Solution: Use 220Ω resistors

  4. Low Power Supply

  5. Insufficient current from power supply

  6. Solution: Use quality 2.5A+ supply

  7. Long Wires

  8. Voltage drop over distance
  9. Solution: Shorter wires or thicker gauge

docs.title: LEDs Flicker
docs.text:
Possible Causes:

  1. Loose Connection
  2. Intermittent contact

  3. Solution: Secure all connections

  4. Power Supply Issue

  5. Voltage instability

  6. Solution: Better power supply

  7. Software Rapid Switching

  8. Code switching too fast
  9. Solution: Add delays between changes

docs.title: Custom Colors (RGB LED)
docs.text:
If using a common cathode RGB LED:

from gpiozero import RGBLED
led = RGBLED(red=22, green=17, blue=27)
# Color mixing
led.color = (1, 0, 0)      # Red
led.color = (0, 1, 0)      # Green
led.color = (0, 0, 1)      # Blue
led.color = (1, 1, 0)      # Yellow
led.color = (1, 0, 1)      # Magenta
led.color = (0, 1, 1)      # Cyan
led.color = (1, 1, 1)      # White

docs.title: Temperature Gradient
docs.text:
Smooth color transitions:

from gpiozero import PWMLED
green_led = PWMLED(17)
yellow_led = PWMLED(27)
red_led = PWMLED(22)
def set_temperature_gradient(temp):
"""Smooth LED transition based on temperature"""
if temp < 18:
green_led.value = 1.0
yellow_led.value = 0.0
red_led.value = 0.0
elif temp < 24:
# Transition green to yellow
ratio = (temp - 18) / 6
green_led.value = 1.0 - ratio
yellow_led.value = ratio
red_led.value = 0.0
elif temp < 30:
# Transition yellow to red
ratio = (temp - 24) / 6
green_led.value = 0.0
yellow_led.value = 1.0 - ratio
red_led.value = ratio
else:
green_led.value = 0.0
yellow_led.value = 0.0
red_led.value = 1.0

docs.title: set_led(color)
docs.text:
Set LED to specified color, turning off all others.

Signature: 

def set_led(color: str) -> None

Parameters:

Parameter Type Description Valid Values color str LED color to activate "green", "yellow", "red", None

Returns: - None

Behavior:

  1. Reset Phase: All LEDs turned off 

    green.off()
yellow.off()
red.off()

  2. Activation Phase: Selected LED turned on 

    if color == "green":
green.on()
elif color == "yellow":
yellow.on()
elif color == "red":
red.on()

  3. Invalid Input: All LEDs remain off

State Transition Table:

Input Color Green LED Yellow LED Red LED "green" ON OFF OFF "yellow" OFF ON OFF "red" OFF OFF ON None OFF OFF OFF Other OFF OFF OFF

Examples:

import actors.led
# Turn on green LED (cool)
actors.led.set_led("green")
# Turn on yellow LED (warm)
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
# Turn on red LED (hot)
actors.led.set_led("red")
# Turn off all LEDs
actors.led.set_led(None)
actors.led.set_led("invalid")  # Also turns all off

Implementation: 

def set_led(color):
"""Sets the LED color based on the input string."""
green.off()
yellow.off()
red.off()
if color == "green":
green.on()
elif color == "yellow":
yellow.on()
elif color == "red":
red.on()

Raises: - No exceptions raised - Invalid colors silently ignored (all LEDs off)

Side Effects: - Modifies GPIO pin states - Changes LED hardware state - All LEDs reset before activation

Performance: - Execution time: < 1ms - GPIO switching: < 100μs per operation

docs.title: selftest()
docs.text:
Run LED self-test sequence to verify hardware functionality.

Signature: 

def selftest() -> None

Parameters: - None

Returns: - None

Behavior:

Cycles through all LED colors with 1-second delays:

  1. Green LED: ON for 1 second
  2. Yellow LED: ON for 1 second
  3. Red LED: ON for 1 second
  4. All OFF: Final state

Total Duration: 3 seconds

Sequence Diagram:

Timeline:  0s    1s    2s    3s
           |     |     |     |
Green:    ████
Yellow:         ████
Red:                  ████

Example: 

import actors.led
# Run hardware test
print("Testing LEDs...")
actors.led.selftest()
print("Test complete")

Implementation: 

def selftest():
"""Runs a self-test by cycling through the LED colors."""
set_led("green")
sleep(1)
set_led("yellow")
sleep(1)
set_led("red")
sleep(1)
set_led(None)

Use Cases:

  1. Initial Setup: Verify wiring during installation
  2. Debugging: Check LED functionality
  3. Startup Routine: Visual confirmation system is ready
  4. Maintenance: Periodic hardware verification

Expected Output: - Visual: All three LEDs should light in sequence - Duration: Exactly 3 seconds - Final state: All LEDs off

Troubleshooting:

Observation Possible Cause Solution No LEDs light No power or wrong pins Check wiring, update pin numbers Only some LEDs work Faulty LED or connection Check specific LED and wiring LEDs very dim Wrong resistor value Use 220Ω resistors Wrong LED lights Pin mismatch Verify GPIO numbers match wiring

docs.text:
import actors.led
def indicate_status(status):
"""Map status to LED color"""
status_map = {
'cool': 'green',
'warm': 'yellow',
'hot': 'red',
'off': None
}

docs.title: Module: sensors.ky001
docs.text:
Temperature sensor module for the KY-001 (DS18B20) digital thermometer.

Module Path: sensors/ky001.py

Import: 

import sensors.ky001
from sensors import ky001

docs.title: base_dir
docs.text:
Base directory for 1-Wire devices.

Type: str

Value: '/sys/bus/w1/devices/'

Description: Linux sysfs path where 1-Wire devices are exposed.

Usage: 

print(sensors.ky001.base_dir)
# /sys/bus/w1/devices/

docs.title: device_folder
docs.text:
Path to the specific DS18B20 device directory.

Type: str

Value: Auto-detected (first device matching 28-*)

Description: Full path to the sensor device folder. The 28- prefix indicates DS18B20 family code.

Usage: 

print(sensors.ky001.device_folder)
# /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-0000091234567

Auto-Detection: 

device_folder = glob.glob(base_dir + '28*')[0]

Multiple Sensors

If multiple DS18B20 sensors are connected, this selects only the first one. Modify code to support multiple sensors.

docs.title: device_file
docs.text:
Full path to the sensor data file.

Type: str

Value: {device_folder}/w1_slave

Description: The actual file read to obtain temperature data.

Usage: 

print(sensors.ky001.device_file)
# /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-0000091234567/w1_slave

docs.title: read_temp_raw()
docs.text:
Read raw data from the temperature sensor file.

Signature: 

def read_temp_raw() -> list[str]

Parameters: - None

Returns: - list[str]: List of two strings containing raw sensor data

Return Value Format: 

[
'f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c : crc=5c YES\n',
'f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c t=31000\n'
]

Line 1: CRC validation result (ends with YES or NO) Line 2: Temperature data (format: t=<temp in millidegrees>)

Raises: - FileNotFoundError: If sensor device file doesn't exist - PermissionError: If insufficient permissions to read file - OSError: On other I/O errors

Example: 

lines = sensors.ky001.read_temp_raw()
print(lines[0])  # CRC check line
print(lines[1])  # Temperature data line

Implementation Details: 

def read_temp_raw():
"""Reads the raw temperature data from the sensor."""
f = open(device_file, mode='r', encoding='utf-8')
lines = f.readlines()
f.close()
return lines

Note: File is opened and closed each time. Consider using with statement for better resource management.

docs.title: read_temp()
docs.text:
Read and process temperature with validation.

Signature: 

def read_temp() -> tuple[float, float]

Parameters: - None

Returns: - tuple[float, float]: Temperature as (celsius, fahrenheit) - Index 0: Temperature in degrees Celsius - Index 1: Temperature in degrees Fahrenheit

Return Value Example: 

(23.5, 74.3)  # 23.5°C, 74.3°F

Raises: - FileNotFoundError: If sensor is not connected - IndexError: If sensor data format is unexpected - ValueError: If temperature cannot be parsed - May hang indefinitely if CRC never validates

Example: 

# Get both units
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
print(f"Temperature: {temp_c}°C ({temp_f}°F)")
# Get only Celsius
temp_c = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]
# Get only Fahrenheit
temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[1]

Algorithm:

  1. Read raw sensor data
  2. Validate CRC (check for 'YES')
  3. If invalid, wait 200ms and retry (infinite loop!)
  4. Parse temperature value from second line
  5. Convert from millidegrees to degrees
  6. Calculate Fahrenheit equivalent
  7. Return tuple

Implementation: 

def read_temp():
"""Uses the read_temp_raw function to get the temperature in Celsius and Fahrenheit."""
lines = read_temp_raw()
while lines[0].strip()[-3:] != 'YES':
time.sleep(0.2)
lines = read_temp_raw()
equals_pos = lines[1].find('t=')
if equals_pos != -1:
temp_string = lines[1][equals_pos+2:]
temp_c = float(temp_string) / 1000.0
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
return temp_c, temp_f

Conversion Formulas:

# Millidegrees to Celsius
temp_c = raw_value / 1000.0
# Celsius to Fahrenheit
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
# Or equivalently
temp_f = (temp_c * 1.8) + 32.0

Known Issues:

Infinite Loop Risk

The validation loop has no timeout: 

while lines[0].strip()[-3:] != 'YES':
time.sleep(0.2)
lines = read_temp_raw()

If sensor is disconnected or malfunctioning, this will hang forever!

Improved Version:

def read_temp_safe(max_retries=10):
"""Read temperature with timeout protection"""
lines = read_temp_raw()
attempts = 0
while lines[0].strip()[-3:] != 'YES':
if attempts >= max_retries:
raise TimeoutError("Sensor CRC validation failed")
time.sleep(0.2)
lines = read_temp_raw()
attempts += 1
equals_pos = lines[1].find('t=')
if equals_pos != -1:
temp_string = lines[1][equals_pos+2:]
temp_c = float(temp_string) / 1000.0
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
return temp_c, temp_f
else:
raise ValueError("Temperature value not found in sensor data")

docs.title: Raw Sensor Output
docs.text:
Format: 

<hex bytes> : crc=<crc_value> <YES|NO>
<hex bytes> t=<temperature>

Example: 

f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c : crc=5c YES
f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c t=31000

Parsing:

# Line 1: CRC validation
is_valid = lines[0].strip().endswith('YES')
# Line 2: Temperature extraction
if 't=' in lines[1]:
start = lines[1].index('t=') + 2
temp_string = lines[1][start:].strip()
temp_millidegrees = int(temp_string)
temp_celsius = temp_millidegrees / 1000.0

docs.title: Temperature Encoding
docs.text:
Temperature is stored as signed 16-bit integer in millidegrees Celsius:

Raw Value Temperature Notes 23500 23.5°C Positive temperature -10250 -10.25°C Negative temperature 0 0°C Freezing point 85000 85°C Power-on reset value

Range: -55000 to 125000 (-55°C to +125°C)

Resolution: 0.001°C (software), 0.0625°C (hardware at 12-bit)

import sensors.ky001

from datetime import datetime
def log_temperature(interval=60, duration=3600):
"""Log temperature to file every interval seconds for duration seconds"""

docs.title: With Error Handling
docs.text:
import sensors.ky001
import time
def read_temperature_safe():
"""Safe temperature reading with error handling"""
max_attempts = 3
for attempt in range(max_attempts):
try:
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
# Validate reasonable range
if -50 <= temp_c <= 100:
return temp_c, temp_f
else:
print(f"Unusual temperature: {temp_c}°C")
except Exception as e:
print(f"Attempt {attempt + 1} failed: {e}")
time.sleep(1)
return None, None
temp_c, temp_f = read_temperature_safe()
if temp_c is not None:
print(f"Temperature: {temp_c}°C")
else:
print("Failed to read temperature")

docs.title: Temperature Filtering
docs.text:
import sensors.ky001
import time
from statistics import mean
def read_temp_filtered(samples=5, interval=0.2):
"""Read temperature with moving average filter"""
readings = []
for _ in range(samples):
temp_c, _ = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
readings.append(temp_c)
time.sleep(interval)
avg_c = mean(readings)
avg_f = avg_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
return avg_c, avg_f
# Get filtered reading
temp_c, temp_f = read_temp_filtered(samples=10)
print(f"Filtered temperature: {temp_c:.2f}°C")

docs.title: Timeout Implementation
docs.text:
import sensors.ky001
import signal
class TimeoutError(Exception):
pass
def timeout_handler(signum, frame):
raise TimeoutError("Sensor read timeout")
# Set timeout
signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, timeout_handler)
signal.alarm(5)  # 5 second timeout
try:
temp_c, temp_f = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
signal.alarm(0)  # Cancel timeout
print(f"Temperature: {temp_c}°C")
except TimeoutError:
print("Sensor read timed out - check connections")

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: This page explains the operational flow and logic of the CPS HHBK temperature monitoring system.

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text:
The CPS HHBK system is a simple yet effective cyber-physical system that bridges the physical world (temperature) with digital control (LED indicators). It operates in a continuous sense-decide-act loop.

graph LR
    A[KY-001 Sensor] -->|Temperature Data| B[Main Controller]
    B -->|Control Signal| C[LED Actors]
    C -->|Visual Feedback| D[User/Environment]
    D -.->|Heat/Cool| A

docs.title: 1. Temperature Sensing
docs.text:
The system continuously reads temperature from the KY-001 sensor via the 1-Wire protocol:

sequenceDiagram
    participant Main
    participant KY001
    participant Hardware

    Main->>KY001: read_temp()
    KY001->>Hardware: read_temp_raw()
    Hardware-->>KY001: Raw data
    KY001->>KY001: Validate (check YES)
    KY001->>KY001: Parse temperature
    KY001->>KY001: Convert to °C and °F
    KY001-->>Main: (temp_c, temp_f)

Key Steps:

  1. Raw Data Read: Opens /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-*/w1_slave to read sensor data
  2. Validation: Checks if the CRC is valid (line ends with "YES")
  3. Retry Logic: If invalid, waits 200ms and retries
  4. Temperature Extraction: Parses the temperature value (format: t=23500 for 23.5°C)
  5. Conversion: Calculates both Celsius and Fahrenheit values

docs.title: 2. Decision Logic
docs.text:
The main controller (main.py) implements a threshold-based decision algorithm:

def get_sensor_state():
temperature = sensors.ky001.read_temp()
if temperature < 21:
actors.led.set_led("green")
elif temperature >= 26:
actors.led.set_led("yellow")
elif temperature >= 31:
actors.led.set_led("red")

Temperature Thresholds

The current implementation has three temperature zones:

  • Cool Zone (< 21°C): Green LED
  • Warm Zone (21-25°C): Yellow LED
  • Hot Zone (≥ 26°C): Red LED

Logic Issue

There's a logical issue in the current implementation - the condition elif temperature >= 31 will never be reached because elif temperature >= 26 catches all values ≥ 26. This should be reviewed and fixed.

docs.title: 3. LED Actuation
docs.text:
The LED controller manages GPIO pins to control LED states:

graph TD
    A[set_led called] --> B[Turn off all LEDs]
    B --> C{Which color?}
    C -->|green| D[Turn on Green GPIO]
    C -->|yellow| E[Turn on Yellow GPIO]
    C -->|red| F[Turn on Red GPIO]
    C -->|None/other| G[All LEDs off]

LED Control Process:

  1. Reset State: All LEDs are turned off first
  2. Selective Activation: Only the target LED is turned on
  3. GPIO Management: Uses gpiozero.LED for clean GPIO abstraction

docs.title: Current Implementation
docs.text:
The current implementation executes a single temperature check followed by a self-test:

# main.py
actors.led.selftest()  # Runs once at startup

docs.title: Continuous Monitoring (Recommended)
docs.text:
For continuous monitoring, implement a control loop:

import time
from main import get_sensor_state
while True:
get_sensor_state()
time.sleep(5)  # Check every 5 seconds

graph TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init[Initialize Hardware]
    Init --> SelfTest[Run LED Self-Test]
    SelfTest --> Loop{Monitoring Loop}
    Loop --> ReadTemp[Read Temperature]
    ReadTemp --> Evaluate[Evaluate Threshold]
    Evaluate --> UpdateLED[Update LED State]
    UpdateLED --> Wait[Wait 5 seconds]
    Wait --> Loop

docs.title: System States
docs.text:
The system can be in one of the following states:

State Temperature LED Status GPIO State Cool < 21°C Green ON GPIO 17: HIGH Comfortable 21-25°C Yellow ON GPIO 27: HIGH Warm ≥ 26°C Red ON GPIO 22: HIGH Self-Test N/A Cycling Sequential activation Idle N/A All OFF All GPIOs: LOW

docs.title: Sensor Errors
docs.text:
The read_temp() function handles sensor errors through validation:

while lines[0].strip()[-3:] != 'YES':
time.sleep(0.2)
lines = read_temp_raw()

If the CRC check fails, the system retries indefinitely until a valid reading is obtained.

Infinite Loop Risk

If the sensor is disconnected or malfunctioning, this creates an infinite loop. Production code should implement a retry limit and error handling.

docs.title: GPIO Errors
docs.text: GPIO errors (e.g., pin already in use, permission denied) are not currently handled and will cause the program to crash.

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    subgraph Physical
        Env[Environment]
        Sensor[DS18B20 Sensor]
        LEDs[RGB LEDs]
    end

    subgraph Software
        KY001[ky001.py]
        Main[main.py]
        LED[led.py]
    end

    subgraph Hardware Interface
        OneWire[1-Wire Bus]
        GPIO[GPIO Pins]
    end

    Env -->|Heat Transfer| Sensor
    Sensor <-->|Digital Signal| OneWire
    OneWire <-->|/sys/bus/w1| KY001
    KY001 -->|temp_c, temp_f| Main
    Main -->|Color Command| LED
    LED <-->|gpiozero| GPIO
    GPIO <-->|Electrical Signal| LEDs
    LEDs -->|Light| Env

docs.title: Performance Characteristics
docs.text:
• Sensor Update Rate: ~1-2 readings/second (limited by DS18B20 conversion time)
  • Response Time: < 1 second from temperature change to LED update
  • Accuracy: ±0.5°C (DS18B20 specification)
  • Resolution: 0.001°C (software), 0.0625°C (hardware)

docs.title: System Design
docs.text: This document describes the architectural decisions, design patterns, and structure of the CPS HHBK system.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The system follows a layered architecture with clear separation of concerns:

graph TB
    subgraph Application Layer
        Main[main.py - Application Logic]
    end

    subgraph Abstraction Layer
        Sensors[sensors/ - Sensor Modules]
        Actors[actors/ - Actor Modules]
    end

    subgraph Hardware Layer
        GPIO[GPIO Interface - gpiozero]
        OneWire[1-Wire Interface - /sys/bus/w1]
    end

    subgraph Physical Layer
        TempSensor[Temperature Sensor]
        LEDHardware[LED Hardware]
    end

    Main --> Sensors
    Main --> Actors
    Sensors --> OneWire
    Actors --> GPIO
    OneWire --> TempSensor
    GPIO --> LEDHardware

docs.title: 1. Separation of Concerns
docs.text: The codebase is organized into three main modules:

docs.title: Sensors Module (sensors/)
docs.text:
Responsibility: Interface with input devices (sensors) and provide abstracted data

# sensors/ky001.py
def read_temp():
"""Returns clean temperature data in both °C and °F"""
# Handles low-level 1-Wire protocol
# Validates data integrity
# Converts raw values to meaningful units

Benefits: - Encapsulates sensor-specific implementation details - Easy to add new sensors without modifying main logic - Testable in isolation

docs.title: Actors Module (actors/)
docs.text:
Responsibility: Interface with output devices (actuators) and control them

# actors/led.py
def set_led(color):
"""Controls LED state based on simple color command"""
# Manages GPIO pin states
# Ensures only one LED is active at a time

Benefits: - Abstracts GPIO complexity from application logic - Centralizes LED control logic - Allows for easy hardware changes

docs.title: Main Module (main.py)
docs.text:
Responsibility: Application logic and decision making

def get_sensor_state():
"""Implements business logic for temperature monitoring"""
# Reads sensor data
# Makes decisions based on thresholds
# Issues commands to actors

Benefits: - Clean, readable business logic - Easy to modify thresholds and behavior - Independent of hardware implementation

docs.title: 2. Abstraction Layers
docs.text:
The system uses abstraction to hide implementation details:

Application Logic (main.py)
        ↓
    "set_led('green')"
        ↓
Actor Abstraction (led.py)
        ↓
    "green.on()"
        ↓
Hardware Library (gpiozero)
        ↓
    GPIO Pin Control
        ↓
Physical Hardware (LED)

docs.title: 3. Single Responsibility Principle
docs.text:
Each module has a single, well-defined purpose:

Module Responsibility Input Output ky001.py Read temperature sensor None Temperature (°C, °F) led.py Control LED states Color string GPIO signals main.py Decision logic Temperature LED commands

docs.title: Project Organization
docs.text:
cps-hhbk/
├── sensors/              # Input device modules
│   ├── __init__.py      # Package initialization
│   ├── sensors.py       # Base sensor abstractions
│   └── ky001.py         # KY-001 specific implementation
├── actors/              # Output device modules
│   ├── __init__.py      # Package initialization
│   ├── actors.py        # Base actor abstractions
│   └── led.py           # LED controller implementation
├── main.py              # Application entry point
├── tests/               # Unit tests
│   └── tests.py
└── docs/                # Documentation

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text:
graph TD
    Main[main.py] --> KY001[sensors.ky001]
    Main --> LED[actors.led]
    KY001 --> Python[Python stdlib: glob, time]
    LED --> GPIOZero[gpiozero library]
    KY001 -.->|reads from| SysFS[/sys/bus/w1/devices/]
    GPIOZero -.->|controls| GPIOPins[GPIO Hardware]

Key Observations:

  • Main module depends on both sensors and actors
  • No circular dependencies
  • External dependencies are minimal (Python stdlib + gpiozero)
  • Hardware access is abstracted through OS interfaces

docs.title: Sensor Interface
docs.text:
Current Interface:

# sensors/ky001.py
def read_temp_raw() -> list[str]:
"""Low-level: Read raw sensor data"""
def read_temp() -> tuple[float, float]:
"""High-level: Get validated temperature in °C and °F"""

Design Decisions:

  • Two-level API: raw data access and processed data
  • Returns tuple for multiple units
  • No parameters needed (sensor location is fixed)

Potential Improvements:

class TemperatureSensor:
"""Base class for temperature sensors"""
def read_celsius(self) -> float:
raise NotImplementedError
def read_fahrenheit(self) -> float:
return self.read_celsius() * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
class KY001(TemperatureSensor):
"""KY-001 specific implementation"""
def __init__(self, device_id: str = None):
self.device_id = device_id or self._auto_detect()

docs.title: Actor Interface
docs.text:
Current Interface:

# actors/led.py
def set_led(color: str) -> None:
"""Set LED to specified color"""
def selftest() -> None:
"""Run LED self-test sequence"""

Design Decisions:

  • String-based color specification (simple but not type-safe)
  • Implicit state management (LEDs are module-level globals)
  • No return value (fire-and-forget command)

Potential Improvements:

from enum import Enum
class LEDColor(Enum):
GREEN = "green"
YELLOW = "yellow"
RED = "red"
OFF = None
class LEDController:
"""LED controller with explicit state management"""
def __init__(self, green_pin: int, yellow_pin: int, red_pin: int):
self.leds = {
LEDColor.GREEN: LED(green_pin),
LEDColor.YELLOW: LED(yellow_pin),
LEDColor.RED: LED(red_pin)
}
self.current_state = LEDColor.OFF
def set_color(self, color: LEDColor) -> None:
"""Set LED color with type safety"""
def get_state(self) -> LEDColor:
"""Query current LED state"""

docs.title: Current Approach
docs.text:
Configuration is hardcoded in source files:

# actors/led.py
green = LED()    # Pin number not specified!
yellow = LED()
red = LED()
# sensors/ky001.py
base_dir = '/sys/bus/w1/devices/'
device_folder = glob.glob(base_dir + '28*')[0]  # Auto-detect first sensor

docs.title: Recommended Approach
docs.text:
Use a configuration file:

config.yamlconfig.py 
gpio:
leds:
green: 17
yellow: 27
red: 22
sensors:
temperature:
type: DS18B20
device_id: "28-0000012345"
bus: w1
thresholds:
cool: 21
warm: 26
hot: 31

import yaml
def load_config(path: str = "config.yaml"):
with open(path) as f:
return yaml.safe_load(f)
config = load_config()

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
• Minimal error handling
  • Infinite retry loops in sensor reading
  • No graceful degradation
  • Crashes on GPIO errors

docs.title: Recommended Strategy
docs.text:
import logging
from typing import Optional
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
class SensorError(Exception):
"""Base exception for sensor errors"""
class SensorTimeoutError(SensorError):
"""Raised when sensor doesn't respond in time"""
def read_temp_safe(max_retries: int = 10) -> Optional[tuple[float, float]]:
"""Read temperature with error handling"""
for attempt in range(max_retries):
try:
return read_temp()
except SensorError as e:
logger.warning(f"Sensor read failed (attempt {attempt + 1}): {e}")
if attempt == max_retries - 1:
logger.error("Sensor read failed after all retries")
return None
time.sleep(0.5)

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
• Stateless sensor reads
  • Implicit LED state (stored in GPIO hardware)
  • No persistence of configuration or state

docs.title: State Diagram
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: System Start
    Idle --> SelfTest: Initialize
    SelfTest --> Monitoring: Test Complete

    Monitoring --> Cool: temp < 21°C
    Monitoring --> Warm: 21 ≤ temp < 26°C
    Monitoring --> Hot: temp ≥ 26°C

    Cool --> Monitoring: Read Next
    Warm --> Monitoring: Read Next
    Hot --> Monitoring: Read Next

    Monitoring --> Error: Sensor Failure
    Error --> Monitoring: Retry Success
    Error --> [*]: Max Retries Exceeded

docs.title: Adding New Sensors
docs.text:
To add a new sensor type:

  1. Create new file in sensors/ directory
  2. Implement read_<data>() function
  3. Import in main.py
  4. Add to decision logic

Example for humidity sensor:

# sensors/dht22.py
def read_humidity() -> float:
"""Read humidity from DHT22 sensor"""
# Implementation
# main.py
import sensors.dht22
def get_sensor_state():
temp = sensors.ky001.read_temp()[0]
humidity = sensors.dht22.read_humidity()
# Decision logic based on both values

docs.title: Adding New Actors
docs.text:
To add a new actor:

  1. Create new file in actors/ directory
  2. Implement control functions
  3. Import in main.py
  4. Add to actuation logic

docs.title: Current Performance
docs.text:
• Sensor Read: ~750ms (DS18B20 conversion time)
  • LED Update: <1ms
  • Decision Logic: <1ms
  • Total Cycle Time: ~750ms

docs.title: Optimization Opportunities
docs.text:
1. Parallel Sensor Reading: Read multiple sensors concurrently
  2. Asynchronous I/O: Use async/await for non-blocking operations
  3. Caching: Cache sensor values with TTL
  4. Batch Updates: Group multiple LED updates if needed

docs.title: Unit Testing
docs.text:
Test each module in isolation:

# tests/test_led.py
import unittest
from unittest.mock import Mock, patch
class TestLEDController(unittest.TestCase):

def test_set_led_green(self, mock_led):

docs.title: Integration Testing
docs.text:
Test module interactions:

# tests/test_integration.py
def test_temperature_to_led_flow():
"""Test complete flow from sensor to LED"""
with patch('sensors.ky001.read_temp') as mock_sensor:
mock_sensor.return_value = (20.0, 68.0)
get_sensor_state()
# Assert green LED was activated

docs.title: Current Risks
docs.text:

docs.title: Recommendations

  4. Logging: Add security event logging

docs.title: Hardware Setup Overview
docs.text: This guide covers the physical setup of the CPS HHBK temperature monitoring system.

docs.title: Core Components
docs.text: Component Quantity Specification Purpose Raspberry Pi 1 Pi 3/4/Zero W Main controller KY-001 Sensor 1 DS18B20 based Temperature sensing Green LED 1 5mm, 2.0V forward voltage Cool indicator Yellow LED 1 5mm, 2.0V forward voltage Warm indicator Red LED 1 5mm, 1.8V forward voltage Hot indicator 220Ω Resistor 3 1/4W LED current limiting 4.7kΩ Resistor 1 1/4W 1-Wire pull-up

docs.title: Additional Materials
docs.text:
• Breadboard (at least 400 tie points)
  • Jumper wires (male-to-male and male-to-female)
  • Power supply for Raspberry Pi (5V, 2.5A minimum)
  • MicroSD card (8GB+) with Raspberry Pi OS

Component Alternatives

  • Any DS18B20-based sensor works (waterproof, bare chip, etc.)
  • LED colors can be customized to preference
  • Resistor values can be adjusted based on LED specifications

docs.title: KY-001 Temperature Sensor
docs.text:
Based on DS18B20 Digital Temperature Sensor

Parameter Specification Operating Voltage 3.0V - 5.5V Temperature Range -55°C to +125°C Accuracy ±0.5°C (-10°C to +85°C) Resolution 9 to 12 bits (configurable) Interface 1-Wire (Dallas/Maxim) Conversion Time 750ms (12-bit resolution)

Pinout:

KY-001 Sensor
┌─────────────┐
│     [S]     │  Signal/Data
│     [+]     │  VCC (3.3V)
│     [-]     │  GND
└─────────────┘

docs.title: LEDs
docs.text:
Standard 5mm LEDs with the following typical specifications:

LED Color Forward Voltage Forward Current Wavelength Green 2.0 - 2.2V 20mA 520-570nm Yellow 2.0 - 2.2V 20mA 585-595nm Red 1.8 - 2.0V 20mA 620-700nm

LED Pinout: 

    LED
    ┌─┐
   ─┤ ├─  Longer leg = Anode (+)
    └─┘   Shorter leg = Cathode (-)

docs.title: Raspberry Pi GPIO
docs.text:
The Raspberry Pi provides:

  • 3.3V GPIO pins for digital I/O
  • 5V and 3.3V power pins for components
  • Ground pins for circuit completion
  • Special function pins including GPIO 4 for 1-Wire

Voltage Warning

Raspberry Pi GPIO pins are 3.3V only. Do not connect 5V signals directly to GPIO pins without level shifters!

docs.title: Recommended Pin Configuration
docs.text: Component GPIO Pin Physical Pin Function KY-001 Data GPIO 4 Pin 7 1-Wire data Green LED GPIO 17 Pin 11 Digital output Yellow LED GPIO 27 Pin 13 Digital output Red LED GPIO 22 Pin 15 Digital output 3.3V Power 3.3V Pin 1 or 17 Power supply Ground GND Pin 6, 9, 14, 20 Common ground

docs.title: Pin Diagram
docs.text:
Raspberry Pi GPIO Header (Top View)
     3.3V [ 1] [ 2] 5V
          [ 3] [ 4] 5V
          [ 5] [ 6] GND
   GPIO 4 [ 7] [ 8]
      GND [ 9] [10]
 GPIO 17  [11] [12]
 GPIO 27  [13] [14] GND
 GPIO 22  [15] [16]
     3.3V [17] [18]
          [19] [20] GND
          ... (continues)

GPIO Numbering

We use BCM (Broadcom) GPIO numbering, not physical pin numbers. Make sure your code uses BCM mode: 

from gpiozero import LED
# gpiozero uses BCM numbering by default
green = LED(17)  # GPIO 17, not pin 17

docs.title: System Power Budget
docs.text:
Component Voltage Current Draw Power Raspberry Pi 3B+ 5V ~500mA idle, 1A active 2.5-5W KY-001 Sensor 3.3V ~1mA ~3mW Green LED 3.3V 20mA ~40mW Yellow LED 3.3V 20mA ~40mW Red LED 3.3V 20mA ~40mW Total 5V ~550-1050mA ~2.6-5.2W

Power Supply Sizing

Use a quality 5V/2.5A (minimum) power supply for Raspberry Pi 3/4. Underpowered supplies cause stability issues, corrupted SD cards, and random reboots.

docs.title: LED Current Limiting
docs.text:
Calculate resistor values for safe LED operation:

R = (Vsupply - Vled) / Iled

For 3.3V supply and green LED (Vf = 2.0V):
R = (3.3V - 2.0V) / 0.02A = 65Ω

Use standard 220Ω for all LEDs (safer, dimmer)
Actual current: I = (3.3V - 2.0V) / 220Ω ≈ 6mA

Resistor Selection

  • 220Ω: Safe, dimmer LEDs (~6-8mA)
  • 150Ω: Brighter LEDs (~10-12mA)
  • 100Ω: Maximum brightness (~15-18mA)

docs.title: Operating Environment
docs.text: Parameter Recommendation Notes Ambient Temperature 0°C to 40°C Raspberry Pi safe range Humidity 20% to 80% RH Non-condensing Ventilation Required Passive cooling adequate Enclosure Recommended Protection from dust/moisture Sensor Placement Away from heat sources Avoid direct sun, heat vents

docs.title: Sensor Placement Guidelines
docs.text:
Do: - ✅ Place in free air for ambient temperature measurement - ✅ Keep away from Raspberry Pi (heat generation) - ✅ Use cable extension if needed (1-Wire supports long cables) - ✅ Mount at desired measurement height

Don't: - ❌ Place near heat-generating components - ❌ Expose to direct sunlight - ❌ Mount in enclosed spaces without ventilation - ❌ Place directly on metal surfaces (thermal coupling)

docs.title: Electrical Safety
docs.text:
Important Safety Rules

  1. Power off before making connections
  2. Check polarity of all components
  3. Verify voltage levels (3.3V vs 5V)
  4. Use current-limiting resistors for all LEDs
  5. Avoid short circuits between power and ground
  6. Don't hot-swap sensors or components

docs.title: ESD Protection
docs.text:
• Use anti-static wrist strap when handling Raspberry Pi
  • Ground yourself before touching components
  • Store components in anti-static bags
  • Work on non-static surface

docs.title: Thermal Management
docs.text:
• Ensure adequate airflow around Raspberry Pi
  • Consider heatsinks for Pi 3/4 under continuous operation
  • Monitor CPU temperature: vcgencmd measure_temp
  • Shutdown if temperature exceeds 80°C

docs.title: Wiring Guide
docs.text:
This guide provides step-by-step instructions for physically connecting all components of the CPS HHBK system.

Safety First

Always power off your Raspberry Pi before making or changing connections!

docs.title: Wiring Overview
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────┐
                    │   Raspberry Pi      │
                    │                     │
    KY-001 Sensor ──┤ GPIO 4 (Pin 7)     │
                    │ 3.3V   (Pin 1)     │
                    │ GND    (Pin 6)     │
                    │                     │
     Green LED ─────┤ GPIO 17 (Pin 11)   │
    Yellow LED ─────┤ GPIO 27 (Pin 13)   │
       Red LED ─────┤ GPIO 22 (Pin 15)   │
                    │                     │
       Common ──────┤ GND (Pin 9, 14, 20)│
                    └─────────────────────┘

docs.title: Step 1: Prepare Your Workspace
docs.text:
1. Gather all components (see Hardware Overview)
  2. Power off Raspberry Pi and disconnect power supply
  3. Set up breadboard near the Raspberry Pi
  4. Organize jumper wires by length and color

Wire Color Convention

Use consistent wire colors for easier debugging: - Red: 3.3V power - Black: Ground - White/Yellow: Data/signal lines - Green/Yellow/Red: Respective LED control signals

docs.title: Step 2: Connect Power Rails
docs.text:
Set up power distribution on breadboard:

Breadboard Power Rails:
────────────────────────────
  + + + + +  Power Rail (+)
────────────────────────────
  - - - - -  Ground Rail (-)
────────────────────────────

Connections:

  1. Connect Raspberry Pi Pin 1 (3.3V) to breadboard + power rail (red wire)
  2. Connect Raspberry Pi Pin 6 (GND) to breadboard - ground rail (black wire)

docs.title: Step 3: Wire the KY-001 Temperature Sensor
docs.text:
Required: - KY-001 sensor module - 4.7kΩ resistor (pull-up resistor) - 3 jumper wires

Connections:

KY-001 Pin Connect To Wire Color Notes VCC (+) Breadboard + rail Red 3.3V power GND (-) Breadboard - rail Black Ground DATA (S) GPIO 4 (Pin 7) Yellow 1-Wire signal

Pull-up Resistor:

Place a 4.7kΩ resistor between: - One end: DATA line (GPIO 4) - Other end: 3.3V power rail

        3.3V
         │
         ├─── 4.7kΩ resistor
         │
    KY-001 DATA ────── GPIO 4

Why Pull-up Resistor?

The 1-Wire protocol requires a pull-up resistor on the data line. The DS18B20 uses open-drain output, which can only pull the line low. The resistor pulls it high when not driven.

Physical Layout:

                    KY-001
                   ┌───────┐
              Red  │ VCC   │
           Yellow  │ DATA  │
            Black  │ GND   │
                   └───────┘
                       │ │ │
       4.7kΩ ─────┐    │ │ │
    3.3V ──────────┴────┘ │ │
    GPIO 4 ────────────────┘ │
    GND ───────────────────────┘

docs.title: Step 4: Wire the LEDs
docs.text: Required per LED: - 1 LED - 1 220Ω resistor - 2 jumper wires

docs.title: LED Orientation
docs.text:
LED Polarity

LEDs are polarized - they only work in one direction!

  • Anode (+): Longer leg, connects to GPIO through resistor
  • Cathode (-): Shorter leg, flat side, connects to ground
      LED Symbol
         ─┬─
        ──┤├──  Triangle points toward ground
          ┴
    Anode (+)  Cathode (-)

docs.title: Green LED Wiring
docs.text:
1. Insert green LED into breadboard
  2. Connect longer leg (anode) to one end of 220Ω resistor
  3. Connect other end of resistor to GPIO 17 (Pin 11)
  4. Connect shorter leg (cathode) to ground rail (-) (black wire)
GPIO 17 ────── 220Ω ────┬──▶│──┬──── GND
                            │
                       Green LED

docs.title: Yellow LED Wiring
docs.text:
1. Insert yellow LED into breadboard
  2. Connect longer leg (anode) to one end of 220Ω resistor
  3. Connect other end of resistor to GPIO 27 (Pin 13)
  4. Connect shorter leg (cathode) to ground rail (-) (black wire)
GPIO 27 ────── 220Ω ────┬──▶│──┬──── GND
                            │
                      Yellow LED

docs.title: Red LED Wiring
docs.text:
1. Insert red LED into breadboard
  2. Connect longer leg (anode) to one end of 220Ω resistor
  3. Connect other end of resistor to GPIO 22 (Pin 15)
  4. Connect shorter leg (cathode) to ground rail (-) (black wire)
GPIO 22 ────── 220Ω ────┬──▶│──┬──── GND
                            │
                        Red LED

docs.title: Step 5: Complete Breadboard Layout
docs.text:
Top view of breadboard:

Power Rails:        Breadboard Layout:

+ ─────────────     GPIO 17 ──┳── 220Ω ──┳──▶│──┬── GND
- ─────────────                            Green

3.3V from Pi        GPIO 27 ──┳── 220Ω ──┳──▶│──┬── GND
                                            Yellow

                    GPIO 22 ──┳── 220Ω ──┳──▶│──┬── GND
                                             Red
KY-001 Module
┌────────┐          GPIO 4 ───┬─────┬── KY-001 DATA
│ VCC    │                    │     │
│ DATA   │          4.7kΩ ────┤     │
│ GND    │                    │     │
└────────┘          3.3V ──────┴─────┴── KY-001 VCC

                    GND ────────────────── KY-001 GND

docs.title: Connection Verification Checklist
docs.text:
Before powering on, verify:

  • All power connections are to 3.3V (not 5V)
  • All ground connections are secure
  • LED polarity is correct (long leg to resistor/GPIO)
  • All resistors are in place (3× 220Ω for LEDs, 1× 4.7kΩ for sensor)
  • GPIO pins match the code configuration
  • No short circuits between power and ground
  • KY-001 sensor connections are correct
  • Pull-up resistor is connected for 1-Wire

docs.title: Visual Inspection
docs.text:
Perform visual inspection:

  1. Check all connections are firm
  2. Look for loose wires on breadboard
  3. Verify wire routing doesn't cross power/ground
  4. Check for solder bridges if using permanent connections
  5. Ensure components are not touching each other

docs.title: Safe Power-Up Procedure
docs.text:
1. Double-check all connections against this guide
  2. Connect Raspberry Pi power supply (do not turn on yet)
  3. Verify no smoke or burning smell
  4. Power on Raspberry Pi
  5. Watch for boot LED on Raspberry Pi
  6. Check for unusual behavior:
  7. Immediate shutdown (short circuit)
  8. No boot (power issue)
  9. Burning smell (stop immediately!)

docs.title: Initial Hardware Test
docs.text:
Once booted, test connections:

# Test 1-Wire sensor detection
ls /sys/bus/w1/devices/

# Should see: 28-XXXXXXXXXXXX (your sensor)

# Test LED control
python3
>>> from gpiozero import LED
>>> green = LED(17)
>>> green.on()   # Green LED should light up
>>> green.off()  # Should turn off

Hardware Ready!

If all tests pass, your hardware is correctly wired! Proceed to Getting Started for software configuration.

docs.title: No LED Response
docs.text: Check: - [ ] LED polarity (flip if needed) - [ ] GPIO pin numbers in code match wiring - [ ] Resistor is present (not blown) - [ ] Connections are firm - [ ] Ground connection is secure

docs.title: Sensor Not Detected
docs.text: Check: - [ ] 1-Wire enabled in raspi-config - [ ] 4.7kΩ pull-up resistor is present - [ ] Data wire connected to GPIO 4 - [ ] Power (3.3V) is connected - [ ] Ground is connected - [ ] No reversed polarity on sensor

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Won't Boot
docs.text: Check: - [ ] Power supply is adequate (2.5A+) - [ ] No short circuit between 3.3V and GND - [ ] Remove all connections and test Pi alone - [ ] SD card is properly inserted

docs.title: Sensors Overview
docs.text: This section documents all sensor modules used in the CPS HHBK system for environmental data acquisition.

docs.title: What Are Sensors?
docs.text:
In the context of cyber-physical systems, sensors are input devices that measure physical properties from the environment and convert them into digital signals that can be processed by the computer system.

graph LR
    A[Physical World] -->|Heat, Light, etc.| B[Sensor]
    B -->|Analog Signal| C[Conversion]
    C -->|Digital Data| D[Software]

docs.title: Temperature Sensors
docs.text: Sensor Type Interface Range Accuracy Status KY-001 DS18B20 1-Wire -55�C to +125�C �0.5�C  Implemented

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
All sensor modules in the sensors/ directory follow a consistent structure:

# sensors/<sensor_name>.py
def read_<data>_raw():
"""Low-level function to read raw data from hardware"""
# Direct hardware access
# Minimal processing
return raw_data
def read_<data>():
"""High-level function with validation and conversion"""
# Calls read_<data>_raw()
# Validates data integrity
# Converts to usable units
return processed_data

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Abstraction: Hide hardware-specific details
  2. Validation: Ensure data quality before returning
  3. Unit Conversion: Return data in standard units
  4. Error Handling: Gracefully handle sensor failures
  5. Documentation: Clear docstrings and comments

docs.title: 1-Wire Protocol (DS18B20)
docs.text:
The KY-001 sensor uses the 1-Wire protocol, a serial communication protocol by Maxim Integrated (formerly Dallas Semiconductor).

Characteristics:

  • Single data wire (plus ground)
  • Multiple devices on same bus
  • Parasitic power option available
  • Built-in addressing for device identification

Implementation:

On Raspberry Pi, 1-Wire is accessed through the Linux kernel driver via sysfs:

/sys/bus/w1/devices/
   28-XXXXXXXXXXXX/
       w1_slave

Data Format:

f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c : crc=5c YES
f0 01 4b 46 7f ff 0c 10 5c t=31000

Line 1: CRC validation (YES = valid) Line 2: Temperature value (t=31000 means 31.000�C)

docs.title: GPIO Interface
docs.text:
Future sensors might use direct GPIO:

  • Digital input (HIGH/LOW)
  • Pulse counting
  • PWM input
  • Interrupt-driven

docs.title: I2C Interface
docs.text:
For advanced sensors (not currently used):

  • Two-wire communication (SDA, SCL)
  • Multiple devices with addressing
  • Higher data rates
  • Common for environmental sensors

docs.title: SPI Interface
docs.text:
For high-speed sensors (not currently used):

  • Four-wire communication
  • Very fast data transfer
  • Common for IMUs, displays

docs.title: Sensor Data Flow
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Phenomenon] -->|Temperature Change| B[DS18B20 Chip]
    B -->|1-Wire Protocol| C[Raspberry Pi GPIO 4]
    C -->|Linux Kernel Driver| D[/sys/bus/w1/devices/]
    D -->|File Read| E[read_temp_raw]
    E -->|Parse & Validate| F[read_temp]
    F -->|Return Data| G[Application Logic]

docs.title: Adding New Sensors
docs.text: To add a new sensor to the system:

docs.title: Step 1: Create Sensor Module
docs.text:
Create a new file in sensors/ directory:

# sensors/new_sensor.py
"""
New Sensor Module
Description of what the sensor measures and how it works.
"""
def read_<measurement>_raw():
"""
    Read raw data from sensor.
    Returns:
        Raw data in sensor-specific format
    """
# Implementation
pass
def read_<measurement>():
"""
    Read processed measurement from sensor.
    Returns:
        float: Measurement value in standard units
    Raises:
        SensorError: If sensor is not responding
    """
# Implementation
pass

docs.title: Step 2: Update Package
docs.text:
Add to sensors/__init__.py:

# sensors/__init__.py
from . import ky001
from . import new_sensor  # Add new sensor
__all__ = ['ky001', 'new_sensor']

docs.title: Step 3: Document
docs.text:
Create documentation file:

# docs/sensors/sensor-<name>.md

docs.title: Step 4: Integrate
docs.text:
Use in main application:

docs.title: Why Calibrate?
docs.text: Sensors may have offsets or drift over time. Calibration ensures accuracy.

docs.title: Offset Calibration
docs.text:
# Add calibration offset
TEMP_OFFSET = -0.5  # Sensor reads 0.5�C too high
def read_temp_calibrated():
temp_c, temp_f = read_temp()
temp_c += TEMP_OFFSET
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
return temp_c, temp_f

docs.title: Two-Point Calibration
docs.text:
For better accuracy across range:

# Known reference points
REF_LOW = 0.0    # Ice water (0�C)
SENSOR_LOW = 0.2  # What sensor reads
REF_HIGH = 100.0  # Boiling water (100�C)
SENSOR_HIGH = 99.5 # What sensor reads
def calibrate(sensor_reading):
"""Apply two-point linear calibration"""
slope = (REF_HIGH - REF_LOW) / (SENSOR_HIGH - SENSOR_LOW)
offset = REF_LOW - (slope * SENSOR_LOW)
return (sensor_reading * slope) + offset

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
# tests/test_sensors.py
import unittest
from sensors import ky001
class TestKY001(unittest.TestCase):
def test_read_temp_returns_tuple(self):
"""Test that read_temp returns (celsius, fahrenheit)"""
result = ky001.read_temp()
self.assertIsInstance(result, tuple)
self.assertEqual(len(result), 2)
def test_temp_conversion(self):
"""Test Celsius to Fahrenheit conversion"""
temp_c = 25.0
temp_f = temp_c * 9.0 / 5.0 + 32.0
self.assertAlmostEqual(temp_f, 77.0, places=1)
def test_temp_in_valid_range(self):
"""Test temperature is within physically possible range"""
temp_c, temp_f = ky001.read_temp()
self.assertGreater(temp_c, -50)
self.assertLess(temp_c, 150)

# Manual hardware test
def test_sensor_hardware():
"""Interactive test for sensor hardware"""
print("Testing KY-001 sensor...")
# Test 1: Can we read data?
try:
temp_c, temp_f = read_temp()
print(f" Sensor reading: {temp_c}�C / {temp_f}�F")
except Exception as e:
print(f" Failed to read sensor: {e}")
return False
# Test 2: Is reading reasonable?
if 0 <= temp_c <= 50:
print(" Temperature in expected range")
else:
print(f" Temperature {temp_c}�C seems unusual")
# Test 3: Multiple readings for stability
readings = []
for i in range(5):
temp_c, _ = read_temp()
readings.append(temp_c)
time.sleep(1)
variance = max(readings) - min(readings)
if variance < 2.0:
print(f" Readings stable (variance: {variance}�C)")
else:
print(f" Readings unstable (variance: {variance}�C)")
return True

docs.title: Do:
docs.text:
• Validate all sensor data before use
  • Implement retry logic for transient failures
  • Log sensor errors for debugging
  • Use type hints for clarity
  • Document expected ranges
  • Handle missing sensors gracefully

docs.title: Don't:
docs.text:
• Trust raw sensor data without validation
  • Ignore error conditions
  • Use infinite retry loops
  • Hard-code sensor paths or IDs
  • Skip data sanitization
  • Ignore outliers

docs.title: Sensor Read Timing
docs.text:
import time
# Measure sensor read time
start = time.time()
temp = read_temp()
duration = time.time() - start
print(f"Sensor read took {duration*1000:.1f}ms")
# Typical: 750-1000ms for DS18B20 at 12-bit resolution

docs.title: Optimization Strategies
docs.text:
1. Lower Resolution: Faster reads, less precision
  2. Caching: Store recent values with TTL
  3. Async Reading: Non-blocking sensor access
  4. Batch Processing: Read multiple sensors concurrently
